title: Renting a Boat in Italy: From the Amalfi Coast to Sardinia
tags: boat rental, yacht charter, italy travel, sailing, amalfi coast
topic: Renting a boat in Italy: from the Amalfi Coast to Sardinia
html:
There is a particular kind of magic to seeing Italy from the water. The coastline that looks crowded and chaotic from a clifftop road suddenly makes sense once you are floating a few hundred metres offshore, watching pastel villages stack themselves up the hillsides. I have spent a fair few summers pottering about the Italian coast, and the single best decision I keep making is to spend at least a couple of days on a boat. If you have ever fancied doing the same, here is an honest guide to how it works, where to go, and what it actually costs to rent a boat for a day or a week.

Why the water beats the road in Italy

Italian coastal roads are gorgeous and, frankly, exhausting. The Amalfi Coast in particular is a single ribbon of tarmac shared by coaches, scooters and very confident locals. From the sea, all of that stress evaporates. You glide past Positano, drop anchor in a quiet cove for a swim, and reach beaches that have no road access at all. The other quiet advantage is timing: you set your own schedule, you eat lunch when you are hungry, and you avoid the midday crush at every famous landmark. For a relaxed afternoon you do not even need a skipper's licence, since plenty of smaller motorboats can be hired without one.

The Amalfi Coast and the Bay of Naples

This is the postcard stretch, and it earns the reputation. A typical day out runs from a base like Sorrento, Amalfi or Salerno, looping past Positano and out towards Capri. The Blue Grotto gets all the attention, but the real joy is the string of swimming spots between the towns, where the water turns that impossible shade of turquoise. If you want to hire a boat here, a small open motorboat for four or five people is the most popular choice, and it keeps the whole thing wonderfully informal. Step it up to a crewed gulet or a sailing yacht and you can add Ischia and Procida to the itinerary without ever queuing for a ferry.

Sardinia and its impossibly clear water

If the Amalfi Coast is about drama, Sardinia is about colour. The water along the Costa Smeralda and the Maddalena archipelago is so clear it almost looks fake in photographs, all pale jade over white sand. The islands of the Maddalena, just off the north coast, are practically designed for a day of island hopping: shallow bays, empty beaches, and short hops between anchorages. A catamaran works beautifully here because the shallow draught lets you nose into coves that deeper hulls cannot reach. Whether you want a bareboat charter or a fully crewed week, it is worth taking time to browse by region so you can compare what is actually available on your dates rather than guessing.

What it costs, roughly

Prices swing with the season, the boat and whether you bring a skipper, but here is a sensible frame of reference for planning.

  • Small motorboat, no licence: often 150 to 350 euros for a half or full day, fuel sometimes extra.
  • Skippered day on a larger boat: 600 to 1,200 euros depending on size and the crew.
  • Bareboat sailing yacht, weekly: roughly 2,000 to 6,000 euros, with high summer at the top end.
  • Crewed catamaran, weekly: 7,000 euros and up, often including a skipper and sometimes a cook.

Two costs catch people out: fuel, which is rarely included on motorboats, and the mooring fees at glamorous marinas, which can be eye watering in August. Build a little buffer into your budget and you will avoid any nasty surprises.

Practical tips before you book

A few things I wish someone had told me earlier:

  1. Book early for July and August. The best boats in the best regions vanish months ahead.
  2. Check whether a licence is required. Engine power and boat length determine this, and the rules are taken seriously.
  3. Read the deposit and fuel policy. Know exactly what you owe at the dock before you sign anything.
  4. Watch the wind. Afternoon breezes pick up in many areas, so morning departures are usually calmer.
The sea does not care about your itinerary, so build in a flexible day. A weather window you almost skipped is often the one you remember for years.

How to find the right boat

The easiest way to compare options across Italy is to search one marketplace rather than emailing twenty separate operators. You can filter by location, dates, crew or no crew, and budget, then message the owner directly. Have a look at one place to find a boat and see what is on offer along the coast. And if you happen to own a boat that sits idle for half the season, the same platform lets you list your boat and earn from it when you are not aboard. However you do it, give yourself a day on the water in Italy. You will spend the rest of the trip wishing you had booked two.
